text: I’m trying to match the exact factory touch-up paint to a specific Lexus color, so the key thing I need is the paint code paired with the official color name. Tables that still list colors but show the codes as numeric values that appear reformatted should be left out because I can’t reliably use them to buy the right touch-up paint. The most useful result would be a table where each row is a specific Alabama football player, and the columns describe that player’s Alabama-related details (like years at the school, position, major honors or milestones). It’s fine if he is just one row among many as long as the table is clearly about players and their Alabama careers. I don’t want tables that list coaching staffs by season, because those are centered on coaches and job titles rather than players’ college careers. I also want to skip general biography fact boxes or timeline-style entries about him, since those don’t present his Alabama career as a player record in a player-by-player list.

text: I’m trying to focus on what his time at the university looked like, in a way that clearly ties him to specific seasons there. A useful table for me should list him alongside other Alabama players and show a span of years for each player’s time at the school, so I can place his college period in context. It really helps when the years are written as an actual range (for example, something like 1962–64) rather than being implied through single one-off events. Tables that only mention one Alabama milestone year for him without showing the full span of his college years don’t give me what I need. I also want to avoid tables that are mainly about coaching staffs or unrelated personal bio facts, since they don’t describe his Alabama playing years in a comparable player list. text: I only want tables exclusively about small-arms bullet caliber-to-diameter conversion, where the entire table is centered on listing calibers and their typical bullet diameters in inches and/or millimeters. The goal is to quickly look up the projectile diameter for a given rifle or handgun caliber without any other ammunition-topic distractions. If a table mixes bullet diameter with unrelated cartridge dimensions (like case length or overall length) or shifts to different measurement systems such as shotgun gauges, it’s not clean enough for my purpose. Tables that mainly discuss pellet sizes, pellet counts, shell loads, or artillery shot classes are also off-topic here because they aren’t about single-bullet diameter by caliber. Only results that stay narrowly focused on bullet diameter vs caliber throughout the table should be included. [SEP] bullet diameter by caliber (inches and mm)

text: I’m trying to pin down the actual dollar amounts people earn under the federal General Schedule system, not a broad overview of federal employment. The only tables that help are those that list pay values by GS grade and step, so I can look up a specific grade/step combination and see the corresponding salary figure. Results should be centered on the GS pay table entries themselves (grades, steps, and their associated pay amounts). Tables that discuss job series, occupational categories, or hiring classifications without giving the grade/step salary amounts don’t meet my need. Likewise, comparisons to other pay systems or rank equivalencies are a distraction here because they don’t provide the GS step-by-step salary figures I’m looking for. [SEP] GS pay scale

text: I’m trying to collect tables that explicitly list formal biological ranks for a taxon, so I can copy the classification hierarchy directly into my notes. The useful results are the ones that clearly name the standard ranks (especially Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, and Family) and fill them with the corresponding classification names. Tables that talk about biology in a descriptive way—like traits, cell types, or general species counts—don’t help because they don’t provide the rank-by-rank taxonomy I need. Broad lists that only cover one rank (for example, just phyla or just families) are also not what I’m looking for, since they don’t show the full hierarchy together. I only want tables where the main content is the explicit rank list itself, not tables where taxonomy is incidental. [SEP] list of biological classification names (kingdom, phylum, class, order, family)

It’s fine if the table also provides a couple of basic company-level descriptors like whether the line is active or where it is based, as long as the focus stays on the companies. I don’t want tables where major columns are mainly describing ship-specific specs or lifecycle details, like when a vessel entered service, its tonnage, or whether that particular ship was scrapped. I also want to avoid schedule-style tables where a whole column is essentially about dates or port calls, because that shifts the focus away from identifying the cruise lines.

text: I’m trying to get an overview of the companies that actually operate cruise vacations, so I can recognize the major brands and where they are based. A useful table for me is one where each row is a single cruise line, with basic details about that company shown in the neighboring columns. It should include fields like whether the line is still operating and where its headquarters are located, so I can compare the operators directly. Tables that list individual ships (with things like tonnage, ship status, or year launched) aren’t what I need, because those rows are ships rather than the cruise lines themselves. I also want to skip port schedules or date-based itineraries that only mention a cruise line as part of a specific sailing, since they don’t provide a clean list of the operators. [SEP] list of cruise lines

text: I’m trying to understand how operating performance connects to earnings before interest and taxes, not just see one metric in isolation. The most useful tables are the ones that put operating income and EBIT in the same view so I can directly compare them for the same period or statement slice. Ideally, the table should make it clear whether these are identical, different, or related, by showing both figures side by side. Tables that only report operating income without EBIT, or only report EBIT without operating income, don’t help me connect the two and should be excluded. Likewise, tables that focus on other profit concepts (like NOPAT or net profit) without presenting both operating income and EBIT are not what I’m looking for. [SEP] operating income and EBIT financial statement

text: I only want tables that are purely about reconciling operating income to EBIT, because I’m trying to pin down the exact bridge between those two measures. Tables that merely list operating income or EBIT as one row among many, without showing how one turns into the other, should be left out. In short, include only tables exclusively centered on the operating-income-to-EBIT linkage. [SEP] operating income and EBIT financial statement

text: I’m trying to pin down the official U.S. Social Security full (normal) retirement age that applies to people based on the year they were born. The useful tables are the ones that directly map a birth year (or birth-year range) to the corresponding FRA/NRA, ideally in years and months. It’s fine if a table also includes closely tied context like how many months there are between age 62 and full retirement age, since that still helps interpret the same schedule. What I don’t want are tables that mainly discuss benefit reduction amounts, delayed retirement credits, or other payout consequences, because those are a different question than the age schedule itself. Tables about non-U.S. programs (for example, another country’s pension age rules) should also be excluded even if they look similar. [SEP] Social Security full retirement age by birth year

Results should be centered on these plant–pest pairings rather than on general gardening lists or broad pest-control categories. Tables that only describe pesticides, chemical substances, or the pests that attack a particular crop without presenting repellent plants are not what I need. Likewise, generic plant catalogs that don’t say what pest is deterred should be excluded. [SEP] pest-repelling plants

text: I’m looking for a practical way to match specific garden pests with the plants that help keep them away, so I can plan what to grow next to what. The most useful tables are the ones where one column is consistently about the plant itself and another column is consistently about which pests it repels, so the relationship is explicit and easy to scan. I also want the pest side to be described as the targets being repelled (like insects or specific species), not as a broad discussion of plant care. Tables that mainly dedicate columns to non-plant categories, like types of pest-control products or general control actions, don’t help me pick companion plants. Likewise, tables that focus on plant attributes such as height, growth type, or resistance ratings without directly tying each plant to the pests it repels should be left out. text: I’m trying to rely on pH information that reflects what the water actually tests at, not what a brand markets on its label. The most useful tables are the ones that report measured pH values from testing, ideally making it clear that the numbers come from a measurement rather than a generic “alkaline/acidic” classification. If a table includes both a measured result and a label or published claim, that’s still fine as long as the measured value is present and central. Tables that only provide marketing-style pH claims, broad ranges for water types, or simplified “good/bad” acidity ratings without an actual tested pH value are not what I want. In short, I want results grounded in measured pH outcomes for specific bottled water brands. [SEP] bottled water brands pH list

text: I’m trying to map specific wireless channel numbers to the exact RF frequencies they correspond to so I can configure equipment and avoid interference. The only tables I want are ones that explicitly list channel identifiers alongside their center frequency and, ideally, the occupied frequency range for that channel. Results that talk about wireless technology in broad terms (like just naming bands such as 5 GHz or 24 GHz) are not useful unless they actually enumerate channel numbers with their frequencies. Tables focused on broadcast TV, public-safety radio, or other non‑Wi‑Fi channel schemes are outside what I need, even if they also use the words “channel” and “frequency”. If a table doesn’t let me directly look up a channel number and read off its corresponding frequency information, it should be excluded. [SEP] wireless channel frequency list

text: I’m trying to quickly map medication categories to the specific drugs that belong to them, so I can recognize which names fall under the same kind of treatment. The most useful tables are the ones that provide reign duration information (for example, days as champion, length of reign, or clearly stated start-and-end points that describe the reign). I want results that connect that reign-length information to the fighters and their weight classes so I can compare how dominant different champions were across divisions. Tables that only list current champions and a “since” date without describing the reign length in any explicit way are not detailed enough for what I’m doing. Likewise, tables that shift the focus to other angles—such as nationality counts, non-UFC organizations, or event-only champion lists—should be excluded.

text: For this topic, I only want tables that are exclusively about title-reign length in the UFC. The table should be centered on reign-duration metrics (like days as champion, longest reigns, or reign length) rather than mixing that information with unrelated themes. If a table primarily catalogs champions by event, lists national totals, or compares multiple MMA organizations—even if it mentions champions—it doesn’t help me isolate reign length. I’m also not looking for simple “current champion since DATE” snapshots unless the table’s main purpose is to quantify the reign itself. In short, unless the table is fully focused on UFC title-reign duration, it’s not what I need. [SEP] UFC champions by division and title reign

text: I’m trying to look up who was holding each belt around that period, and I want to be able to scan across weight classes without digging into individual fight narratives. The most useful tables to me are the ones where at least one column is consistently about the weight class or division itself, and another column is consistently about who the champion is for that class. It also really helps when there’s a column that conveys the timing of the reign in a comparable way across rows, like when it started or how long it has lasted. I don’t want tables where the main columns are about specific events, venues, or match results, because that turns it into a fight-by-fight log rather than a champions-by-division view. I’d also rather avoid tables that focus on countries or aggregate leaderboards, since they don’t directly map each division to its current titleholder and reign timing. [SEP] UFC champions by division and title reign

text: I’m trying to get a clear snapshot of who holds each UFC men’s belt and how long they’ve been in that current run. Also skip tables that summarize counts by country or global rankings like “longest reigning” lists, since they don’t give a simple division-by-division current reign picture. text: What I need is a clean lookup for the DST changeover dates—nothing else. I only want tables that are exclusively about when DST starts and when it ends for given years (and possibly the local clock time of the switch). If a table shifts the focus to time-zone names, UTC offsets, or other background timekeeping information, it becomes noisy and I don’t want it. Likewise, country-level overviews that are mainly about whether a place observes DST rather than listing yearly start/end dates aren’t suitable. The best matches are tables where the entire content is just the yearly DST begin/end schedule. [SEP] daylight saving time start and end dates by city and year

text: I’m trying to pin down exactly when the clock changes happened for specific places, so I can line up the switch dates across different years. The most useful tables for me are the ones where some columns consistently describe the actual start moment and the actual end moment of the seasonal clock shift for the same location, year by year. It helps if those columns spell out the calendar date and, when available, the local time of day when the change takes effect, because that’s what I need to compare. I don’t want tables where the main columns are about time-zone abbreviations or offsets, since that doesn’t tell me the start/end change dates for a city. I also want to avoid tables whose columns are mostly about countries or broader regions rather than a single city-level place, because that’s too coarse for what I’m doing. [SEP] daylight saving time start and end dates by city and year

text: I’m trying to compare refinancing costs across different parts of the U.S., so I can see how the same type of refinance loan looks in one place versus another. The most useful tables are the ones where at least one column is clearly devoted to U.S. locations (like a state) so the numbers can be read as state-by-state entries. It also helps if the rest of the table stays focused on refinance offers for common loan products, with the rate information lined up next to each location. I’m not looking for general national rate sheets that only list loan programs without tying them to any state. And tables that mainly describe how adjustable-rate loans evolve over time (years, resets, caps, balances) don’t help me make a geographic comparison, so I want to exclude those even if they mention mortgage rates. text: I’m trying to identify which countries have formally committed to a particular international legal instrument, not just learn general facts about international organizations or historical events. The tables I want should explicitly list countries and indicate their legal participation status in a treaty or convention, such as being a state party, having signed, or having ratified. Results are useful when they let me match a country to a clear treaty-related status (and possibly a date), so I can tell who is actually bound or has taken concrete steps toward joining. Tables that only give aggregate counts of states, membership totals, or other summary statistics without naming the countries don’t help me. Tables that are mainly about unrelated UN resolutions, background history, or organizational profiles should also be excluded even if they are tangentially connected to international law. [SEP] countries party to international treaties and conventions

text: I’m trying to use this information the way a customer would when exchanging money through a bank or bank-like service. The tables I want should show the rates a bank actually offers to clients, such as buying vs. selling rates, cash/TT distinctions, or any stated commission or spread. It’s important that the numbers reflect what the bank pays you versus what it charges you, not just a single interbank or reference quote. Tables that only list one generic exchange rate per currency pair without a buy/sell side, or that focus on currency symbols/codes, are not useful for my purpose. I also want to exclude tables that use exchange-rate figures only as inputs for accounting or hedging examples rather than presenting bank-offered rates. [SEP] bank currency exchange rates

text: I’m trying to get a clearer picture of a team’s overall cap situation, not a list of what individual players make. Results should help me compare what the team is carrying against the cap, ideally year-by-year, so I can see how tight or flexible the cap is. Tables that mainly enumerate players with their base salaries, average annual values, or contract terms don’t answer this higher-level question. Likewise, offseason transaction lists that show a few signings and their contract lengths are too granular and incomplete for what I need and should be excluded. For each of those vehicle rows, I want the key wheel specs shown as attributes, especially the bolt pattern/PCD and the rim spec details like size and offset (ET) when available. Tables that only list a wheel or tire size without clearly naming the exact vehicle and its year (or year range) aren’t useful, because I can’t tell what car the numbers apply to. I also want to exclude tables that focus only on hardware like stud size or vague offset descriptions without giving actual rim and fitment specs for the vehicle. [SEP] wheel and tire sizes (rim specs, bolt pattern/PCD, offset) by car model and year

If the dates are split into multiple separate days with commas or ampersands, I have to mentally stitch them together and it’s harder to compare conferences quickly. I’m also not looking for the broader national tournament game-by-game TV slate, since that’s a different kind of schedule. And tables that talk about champions or footnotes without giving the tournament date range don’t really help for planning. [SEP] NCAA basketball conference tournament schedule

text: I’m trying to get a clean list of the major roads in this state so I can quickly see which highways exist and what corridors they run along. I’m fine if the table also includes a stable reference-style column (like a mapping/database identifier), as long as it still stays focused on the highways themselves. What I don’t want are tables that devote any main column to vehicle administration items like registration codes or district office details, because that’s not a highway list. I also want to exclude tables where the columns are mainly about city-level economic or infrastructure indicators rather than describing highways and their routes. text: I’m trying to look at actual on-exchange trading activity, not a general snapshot of a quote page. The most useful results are tables that list individual intraday updates as a sequence of records, where each row corresponds to a specific time (or time/date) along with the executed price and the associated trade volume. This kind of tick-by-tick feed is what lets me see how the price moved minute by minute and how much stock changed hands at each moment. Tables that only show a single current price, previous close, bid/ask, day’s range, or market cap don’t provide that trading timeline and shouldn’t be included. Likewise, index ranking or summary “key stats” tables are not what I’m after here. [SEP] London Stock Exchange share price (time, price, volume)

What’s most useful to me is a table where each row is a European country and it explicitly states whether capital punishment has been abolished, and in what year that abolition happened. If the table only lists individual executions, specific people, or military cases, it doesn’t help me compare countries at a glance. Likewise, tables that focus on opinion polls or other side topics are not what I’m looking for because they don’t directly show the legal status by country. I want to filter out anything that is organized around executed persons or survey years rather than around countries with an abolition year.

text: I’m trying to interpret shorthand that shows up specifically in medication contexts, like on prescriptions, MARs, or pharmacy labels. The tables I want should map those brief codes to their intended dosing schedule, timing, route/administration cues, or formulation-related notes that affect how a drug is taken. It’s fine if the abbreviations come from Latin-style prescription conventions or common pharmacy usage, as long as the meaning is clearly tied to medication use. Tables that are mainly about diseases, lab tests, anatomy, procedures, or general clinical terms aren’t helpful for this task, even if they are legitimate medical abbreviations. I only want results that help me decode medication instructions, not broader clinical vocabulary. [SEP] medical abbreviation meanings

text: I’m trying to compare how much the same institution charges residents versus non-residents, and I need the pricing split to be explicit. The most useful tables are those that present both in-state and out-of-state tuition and/or mandatory fees for a given school in a way that allows a direct side-by-side comparison. Results are still relevant if they break the price into pieces (tuition, flat fees, per-credit charges) as long as the resident vs non-resident distinction is clearly stated. Tables that only give a single tuition number without indicating residency are not helpful for this comparison. Tables that discuss costs like room and board, books, or other living expenses instead of focusing on the tuition-and-fee amounts should be excluded. [SEP] college tuition and fees by school (in-state and out-of-state)

I’d also rather skip tables that only show a single sticker price or general “cost of attendance” totals without keeping the resident and non-resident tuition/fee amounts clearly comparable. If a table mixes in aromatics/fluorinated compounds, or switches to properties like melting point, density, or isomer counts as a parallel topic, it becomes harder to isolate what I’m comparing. I’m also not looking for product ranges (like gasoline) where the boiling point is a broad interval rather than a compound’s value. Anything that is not purely centered on n-alkane boiling points should be left out.

I don’t want tables where one of the main columns is about unrelated physical properties like density or specific gravity, since that pulls attention away from boiling behavior. And I’m not looking for tables that mainly describe things like fuel blends, generic products, or halogenated/aromatic compounds, even if they happen to list a boiling point somewhere. [SEP] compare boiling points of alkanes (hydrocarbons)

It’s fine if the table is a simple ranking or leaderboard, as long as the focus is on singles titles won. Tables about reaching finals (including consecutive-finals streaks) without clearly giving title totals don’t answer what I need. Likewise, schedules, tournament categories, doubles results, wheelchair categories, or one-off match listings should be excluded because they are not about singles title winners.

text: I only want tables that are exclusively about Grand Slam singles title winners and their total title counts. The table should primarily be a winners leaderboard: player names paired with how many singles majors they’ve won. If a table shifts attention to finals appearances, match-by-match finals details, tournament schedules, event classifications, or other disciplines (like doubles or wheelchair), it becomes too mixed for my purpose. Even if it mentions a Grand Slam tournament, it’s not useful unless the central content is the singles champion title tally. I’m trying to keep the results clean, so anything that is not purely about singles title totals should be left out. [SEP] tennis Grand Slam singles title winners (men and women)

text: I’m trying to pull tables that help me identify who performed which on-screen roles for the title listed, so I can quickly match performers to the characters they portray. The most useful results are tables where at least one column is consistently about the actors/performers themselves, and another column is consistently about the fictional roles or character names they played. If the title is a series or episode, it’s fine if the role descriptions include things like how many episodes they appear in, as long as the focus stays on performer-to-role mapping. I’m not looking for tables where the columns mainly describe behind-the-scenes jobs or production departments like directing, writing, editing, or other crew responsibilities. If a table has columns whose main content is production roles rather than on-screen characters, it should be excluded even if it comes from an IMDb “cast & crew” page. [SEP] IMDb full cast and crew

text: I’m trying to pin down what people in Sri Lanka would actually pay for gold right now, not a generic international spot quote. The only tables that help are those that report Sri Lanka–specific prices in Sri Lankan Rupees and that clearly break the rate out by karat, especially 24K and 22K on a per‑gram basis. A table is still useful if it includes a date stamp and a few extra Sri Lanka units (like ounce or a local weight), as long as the per‑gram 24K and 22K figures are present. Tables about other countries’ gold prices, even if they list 24K/22K per gram, don’t answer the local question and should be excluded. Likewise, global USD spot-price tables without a Sri Lanka context or without the 24K vs 22K split are not what I’m looking for. [SEP] gold price in Sri Lanka today (24K and 22K per gram)

text: I’m trying to compare candidate materials specifically from the perspective of how they will behave as a spring under load. The most useful tables are the ones that report mechanical properties tied to elastic response and strength, such as modulus of elasticity (or tensile modulus), yield/ultimate tensile strength, hardness, and ductility/elongation. Results should help me judge stiffness and whether a material will permanently deform, not just identify a material family. Tables that only list chemical composition, classification labels, or historical/type descriptions without mechanical property values are not what I need. Even if the material is commonly used in engineering, it should be excluded if the table doesn’t actually provide spring-relevant mechanical properties. [SEP] common spring materials and properties

The table should read like a direct lookup: named plane shapes paired with their rotational symmetry order, without drifting into wallpaper groups, tilings, point groups, or 3D polytope symmetry classifications. If the content is primarily about symmetry groups in three dimensions, compounds/polytopes, or notation for rotation centers in patterns, it’s not what I’m after. Even if such tables include the word “order,” they’re mixing in a different topic and make it harder to find the simple shape-by-shape answers. Keep the results tightly focused on 2D shape entries and their rotational symmetry order only.

text: I’m trying to quickly compare common flat shapes by how many times they match up with themselves during a full turn. The most useful tables for me are ones where at least one column is consistently describing that rotation “count” for each listed 2D shape, so I can scan down and compare shapes side by side. It also helps if the table sticks to ordinary plane shapes rather than moving into 3D solids, polyhedra, or abstract symmetry groups. I don’t want tables where whole columns are mainly about group names, high-dimensional objects, or long narrative explanations of symmetry operations, because that makes it hard to just read off the rotation behavior. If the table’s columns are largely describing 3D point-group structure, tilings, or compound polytope classifications instead of planar shapes and their rotation counts, I’d rather leave it out. [SEP] order of rotational symmetry of 2D shapes

text: I’m studying common flat shapes and I want a clear way to compare how many times each one maps onto itself when rotated. The most useful tables for me are the ones where each row is a single 2D shape (like a triangle, kite, or trapezium) and one of the columns explicitly gives the order of rotational symmetry for that shape. It’s fine if the same row also lists other basic symmetry facts, such as the number of reflection axes or a total symmetry count, as long as the rotational order is stated per shape. I don’t want tables that are mainly about 3D symmetry groups, polytopes, or point groups, because those are not individual plane shapes with a simple rotational order entry. I also want to exclude tables that only explain rotation-center symbols or tiling patterns without listing specific 2D shapes as the row items, since they don’t let me look up a shape directly. text: I’m trying to build a clean cross-reference between common pose names and their traditional Sanskrit equivalents. It’s fine if the table also includes light pose metadata like the pose category/type, as long as the core content is still the name mapping. Tables that only list English names without the Sanskrit counterparts don’t help, because I can’t confirm the traditional term. Likewise, tables that discuss broader yoga philosophy, limbs, principles, or non-pose concepts should be excluded even if they contain Sanskrit words. [SEP] yoga pose names in English and Sanskrit

text: I’m trying to type special characters on a Windows keyboard using numeric Alt key sequences, and I need tables that directly map an Alt+number code to the symbol it produces. The useful results are those that present the actual Alt-code forms (like Alt 0128, Alt 0152, etc.) alongside the resulting character and, ideally, a short description of what that character is. Tables that only list base/extended ASCII values in decimal/octal/hex without showing the Windows Alt keystroke codes don’t help me apply this in practice. Likewise, tables about encoding schemes or representations (like uuencoding or protocol dumps) are not what I’m looking for because they don’t tell me what to press to get a symbol. Any table that focuses on unrelated notations or character systems rather than Windows Alt codes should be excluded. [SEP] ASCII alt codes for symbols

text: I’m trying to compare how much debt different U.S. states are carrying so I can see which ones are relatively more indebted. The most useful result is a table where each row is a single state, and the columns give that state’s debt amounts, such as total state debt and ideally a per-person figure. I want something that lets me scan multiple states side by side in the same layout, not a breakdown of one state’s budget sources. Tables about consumer or credit-card debt rules (like statute of limitations by state) aren’t what I’m after, because those are legal time limits rather than the state government’s debt. I also don’t want tables where the main entries are revenue categories or other non-state items, since that doesn’t help me compare states’ debt directly. [SEP] US state debt by state

I want to be able to compare different benefit options side by side based on what they pay or how eligibility/payout is calculated. Tables that mainly use their columns to map ranks, pay grades, or civilian-to-military equivalencies don’t help me understand retirement benefits themselves. Likewise, tables focused on general public programs or age-based retirement rules that aren’t specifically about military or veterans compensation should be excluded.

text: I’m trying to understand the different kinds of military retirement pay options, and what each one is actually based on when the payment is calculated. The most useful tables are the ones where each row is a specific retirement plan name, and the columns describe what that plan uses as its basis (for example, final basic pay versus an average of certain months). I want it to stay focused on retirement pay plan types themselves, not general pay grades or rank equivalency charts. Tables that mainly list active-duty salaries by rank or compare military ranks to civilian grades won’t help, because they aren’t explaining retirement plan calculations. I also want to exclude disability compensation rate tables that are organized by dependent status or percentage ratings, since those are a different kind of benefit and not a retirement plan breakdown. [SEP] military retirement benefits and compensation

text: I’m trying to compare the main kinds of military-related retirement and disability compensation, but I need it organized in a way that clearly separates one plan from another. Tables that focus on ranks, pay grades, or civilian equivalencies are about military pay in general, but they don’t actually lay out the retirement plan options themselves. Tables that only list benefit amounts by dependency status without identifying the underlying retirement plan should also be left out, because I can’t tell which plan rules those numbers come from. text: I’m trying to get a clean picture of which places sit at the very bottom of the population ladder. The most useful tables are ones that list the least populous countries or dependent territories and give their population figures in a smallest-to-larger ordering (or at least clearly indicate they are the smallest entries). I only want content that helps identify these tiny-population places directly, not broad global rankings where the excerpted rows are mostly large-population countries. Tables limited to a specific demographic breakdown (like age structure over time) don’t help, because they don’t answer the “which places are smallest” question. Likewise, region-specific lists are only useful if they actually highlight the smallest entities, rather than just giving a mid-range regional ranking. [SEP] countries and territories by population (smallest to largest)

text: I’m trying to compare places purely by how many people live there, starting from the tiniest populations and moving upward. The most useful tables for me are the ones where at least one column is consistently about the size of the population for each place, so I can scan and order them by that figure. I’m fine if the list is global or limited to a region, as long as it’s still comparing countries or territories against each other in that same population-focused way. What I don’t want are tables where columns mainly describe population structure, like age-group shares or demographic breakdowns, because that changes the focus from “how big” to “who makes it up.” If a table puts substantial emphasis on those demographic composition columns rather than straightforward population totals, it’s not a good fit for what I’m trying to do. text: I’m trying to pin down a clean, chronological roster of who served as the top colonial governor in New France and when they held that office. When a table starts framing the information as a broader lineup (for example, a season overview or a character/title directory), it stops being the clean episode-only cast reference I need. Even if those broader tables mention the same names, they dilute the purpose and make it harder to confirm who was credited in that episode. So unless the table is fully centered on episode-specific cast/roles and nothing else, it isn’t suitable. [SEP] Law & Order: Special Victims Unit episode cast

text: I’m trying to map knitting needle diameters in millimeters to the corresponding numbering systems used in different countries so I can match a pattern written in one system with needles labeled in another. The only tables that help are those that explicitly present knitting needle size equivalences between metric (mm) and US and/or UK needle numbers. If a table is mainly about crochet hooks, or mixes crochet sizing with needle sizing in a way that makes the needle conversion feel secondary, it isn’t what I’m looking for. Likewise, tables that convert millimeters to inches without referencing needle sizes don’t answer the practical question I have. Please keep results focused on needle-number-to-mm equivalences and drop the rest. [SEP] knitting needle size conversion chart in mm (US/UK/metric)

text: I’m trying to quickly identify landscaping choices that deer are least likely to browse, so I can pick plants with the strongest track record of being left alone. The most useful tables for me are those that explicitly rate or rank individual landscape plants by deer resistance. In particular, I want results that let me focus on the top tier of resistance—entries clearly labeled as the highest category (for example, an “A” or equivalent “most resistant” rating). Tables that only list plant names without any deer-resistance rating don’t help me make a confident selection. Also, general wildlife diet or seasonal foraging tables are not what I need, because they describe deer behavior rather than giving plant-by-plant recommendations for a garden plan. [SEP] deer resistant landscape plants

text: I’m trying to compare how long a creditor or plaintiff has to sue across different U.S. states when the claim is based on a contract or consumer debt. The most useful tables are the ones that break the time limits out by contract type, especially distinguishing oral agreements from written contracts (and similar debt categories). I want results where that oral-versus-written distinction is central to the table, so I can quickly scan states and see the differences. Tables that list statutes of limitations for unrelated causes of action—like personal injury, libel, malpractice, or criminal offenses—don’t help for this purpose even if they mention time limits. Likewise, tables that focus on only one state’s general civil limitations without highlighting contract/debt categories should be excluded. [SEP] statute of limitations by state for written and oral contracts or debt

The most useful tables for me are the ones where at least a couple of columns consistently express an official quote or parity between currencies (for example a base currency and its official value in another currency), so the numbers clearly represent an exchange rate level. I also want tables where the columns are centered on the rate itself across time points or across multiple currencies, so I can scan and compare the official figures quickly. What I don’t want are tables that devote columns to broader macroeconomic indicators or policy frameworks alongside the rate, because that mixes the exchange rate with unrelated measures. And I also want to avoid tables where the main columns are about hedging/derivatives valuation or accounting changes, since those figures aren’t the official rates I’m trying to collect.

text: I’m trying to find authoritative reference rates that are published by official bodies, so I can quote them with confidence. The most useful result is a table where each row is one currency (like USD, EUR, GBP, or SDR), and the columns show the official rate(s) for that currency, such as buying/selling/central or similar published categories. I want the table to be centered on the currencies themselves, not on a timeline. Please avoid tables where the rows are years or dates and the values just show how one currency pair changed over time, because that makes it hard to compare many currencies at once. Also exclude tables that are about hedging/accounting, GDP/macroeconomic indicators, or policy frameworks, even if they contain an exchange-rate column, since those are not official rate lists by currency. [SEP] official currency exchange rates by central banks and international organizations

text: I’m trying to pull an official set of reference rates that an authority publishes, so I can see how different currencies are priced against a base. What matters to me is that each currency is broken out as its own row, since I want to scan across multiple currencies side by side. The table should look like an official rate sheet where the currency names are listed and the corresponding rate columns sit next to them. If the rates are instead organized by year or date across the columns, it becomes hard for me to compare currencies at a single point in time. Those time-series layouts are still about exchange rates, but they are not the format I’m looking for right now. [SEP] official currency exchange rates by central banks and international organizations

text: I’m trying to compare how pay differs across academic credentials within the context of a particular school, not just get generic salary facts. The most useful tables are those that list a university and then break down average pay by degree level or major (for example, BA vs MBA vs PhD, or specific majors under those degrees). Results should clearly tie the salary figures to that institution and present multiple degree entries so I can compare them side by side. Tables that only show salaries for job titles, occupations, or national career medians—even if they mention a major—don’t help with this school-by-degree comparison. Likewise, general job rankings or industry salary change tables should be excluded because they aren’t organized around a university’s degree breakdown. [SEP] university salary by degree average salaries

text: I’m trying to pin down the IPv4 blocks that are set aside for special use beyond ordinary public addressing, especially the ranges that are reserved or designated for a specific technical role. The most useful tables are the ones that list those non-RFC1918 special-use blocks (for example loopback, shared address space, or “this network”) together with their CIDR/range and what they are meant to be used for. I care about the intent/scope of the reservation, not general IPv4 background. Tables that only cover the classic private LAN ranges used inside organizations are not what I need here. Likewise, generic subnet-mask/CIDR cheat sheets or classful-addressing charts should be excluded because they don’t identify reserved blocks and their purpose. [SEP] IPv4 private and reserved IP address ranges (RFC 1918 and related)

text: I want tables exclusively about the non-RFC1918 special-use/reserved IPv4 ranges and what each block is reserved for. text: I’m trying to quickly identify which IPv4 blocks are meant to be used internally or otherwise set aside so they won’t be routable on the public internet. The most helpful tables are the ones where some columns consistently describe each block’s actual address span and what the block is meant for, so I can tell private-use ranges apart from other special-purpose ones. I also need the table to have at least one column that clearly explains the intended use or scope of each block (for example whether it’s private, loopback, shared space, etc.), not just the raw numbers. What I don’t want are tables whose columns are mainly about subnetting mechanics, like translating masks and prefixes or counting how many addresses a generic prefix has. Those kinds of subnet-mask cheat sheets are useful for a different task, but they don’t help me enumerate the reserved/private ranges themselves. [SEP] IPv4 private and reserved IP address ranges (RFC 1918 and related)

I also want to avoid tables that focus on address classes (Class A/B/C) rather than listing the actual reserved/private ranges themselves. text: I’m trying to put together a clear list of the IPv4 blocks that are not meant for normal public Internet routing, so I can quickly recognize them when I see an address. For that to be useful, I need each entry shown in CIDR form (like something/8 or something/12), not just as a start–end range. Tables that also include what the block is used for (private, loopback, shared space, etc.) are fine, but the CIDR block has to be explicitly present as its own field. If a table only lists ranges like “10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255” without giving the prefix length, it slows me down because I have to convert it myself. So please focus on tables that present these special-use ranges directly as CIDR blocks. [SEP] IPv4 private and reserved IP address ranges (RFC 1918 and related)

text: I’m trying to connect individual inherited conditions to the specific genetic location that explains them. The most useful tables are the ones that explicitly name a causative gene and/or a precise chromosomal locus (such as a band location), so I can map each condition to a concrete genetic association. Tables are relevant when they provide condition-to-gene or condition-to-locus linkages, even if they also include contextual identifiers like OMIM entries or inheritance patterns. Tables that only group disorders into broad categories, list screening test markers, or enumerate aneuploidy types without tying each item to a specific gene or locus don’t help me. I want results that let me point to the exact gene or chromosomal location involved, not just a general classification or diagnostic context. [SEP] list of genetic disorders and their associated genes or chromosomes

text: I’m trying to compare cooking oils based on what their fats are actually made of, so I can pick oils with a fat profile that fits my cooking and diet preferences.
